text: A forty-year-old woman presented to the emergency department with forty-eight hours of abdominal pain, nausea and constipation, with no signs of peritonitis. She referred similar symptoms one year before, spontaneously resolved after a couple of days. She had a CT scan done in another hospital twenty-four hours earlier, showing bowel obstruction due to ileocecal interception. Urgent colonoscopy was performed. About fifteen centimetres of terminal ileum was invaginated through the ileocecal valve with no signs of ischemia.

During the emergency laparoscopy, the surgeon attempts to reduce the ileocecal intussusception laparoscopically, using standard trocar positions for a right colectomy. The bowel appears dilated at the distal ileum, while the remaining small and large bowel show good perfusion without tension.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral ileocolic resection, stretching the ileocecal junction to identify and divide the ileocolic vessels. The dissection plane is anterior to the congenital layer of peritoneum, lying over the retroperitoneum, duodenum, and ureter. The dissection progresses upward along Toldt's fascia towards the hepatic flexure.

The surgeon dissects the anterior and right aspects of the duodenum, sealing and dividing the thin right colic vessels using ultrasonic energy. The hepatic flexure is mobilized, and the area of previous dissection is joined. The site for colon resection is determined through laparoscopic palpation.

The surgeon uses a linear stapler to transect the transverse colon. The cecum, appendix, and distal ileum are dissected away from retroperitoneal structures. The small bowel mesentery is sealed using ultrasonic energy, and the ileum is divided. A complete intracorporeal side-to-side staple anastomosis is performed with a double-layer closure of the enterotomy.

The specimen was extracted through a five centimeter funnestal incision. Histology revealed benign ileocolic interception with mucosal and submucosal edema, pseudomembranous colitis involving the cecum and inflammatory debris in the terminal ileum with thirty reactive lymph nodes. The patient recovered well and was discharged home on day six.
